text: The indication for surgery was a case of haematomatous polyposis of the colon causing protein-losing enteropathy with severe hypoalbuminia and anisarca was built up nutritionally before taking her up for surgery.
The surgeon begins the colectomy by mobilizing the right side of the colon after inspecting the peritoneal cavity and colon.
The surgeon uses the left hand instrument to lift the ileocecal junction, causing tenting of the ileocolic vessels as part of the medial to lateral mobilization of the right colon.

The surgeon makes an incision on the right colonic mesentery, entering the loose areolar tissue plane between the colonic mesentery and the retroperitoneum, following traction on the ileocolic vessels.
The surgeon develops the avascular plane between the right colon mesentery and the retroperitoneum using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection techniques, visibly separating the tissue layers.
In the lower abdomen, the surgeon identifies the right ureter, gonadal vessels, and iliac vessels, which are covered by a layer of retroperitoneal fat.
The surgeon continues cranial dissection with the duodenum visible medially and the gyrotas positioned laterally, advancing the mobilization of the right colon.

The surgeon encounters the duodenum and carefully avoids entering the retro-duodenal plane, staying anterior to the duodenum. The mesenteric attachment to the duodenum and the head of the pancreas is divided as the dissection progresses superiorly.
The surgeon identifies the white line laterally, which separates the retroperitoneal geratous fascia from the colonic mesentery, and proceeds to separate the tissues along this plane.
This is a very important advantage of doing the resection in a medial to lateral
technique because sometimes if we do in the lateral to medial technique, there's always a risk that you may accidentally enter the plane behind the geratous fascia or proceed retrorheodinarily.
The complete C-loop of the duodenum and the head of the pancreas are visible medially as the surgeon separates the mesenteric attachment from these structures.

The surgeon incises the right colon mesentery above the iliocolic vessels, then clips and divides the iliocolic vessels after separating them from the peritoneum and clearing the surrounding lymph nodes.
If the colectomy is done for a right colon malignancy we go to the origin of the ileocolic vessels on the supramyocentric artery but in this case for polyphosis we can go a bit

Likewise, if we are doing a total proctocolectomy with a plan for a J pouch, we don't ligate the iliocollic vessels at all and in fact take the colonic branches of the iliocollic artery close to the colon.
The surgeon begins mobilizing the hepatic flexure, carefully separating it from surrounding tissues to facilitate further dissection.
The camera port is repositioned from the suprapubic port to the umbilical port, while downward traction is applied to the right colon at the hepatic flexure, enhancing the view of the surgical field.
The surgeon divides the duodenocolic ligament and the anterior mesocolic attachment on the duodenum, revealing the plane that joins the previously created retroperitoneal tunnel.
The surgeon divides and separates the lateral attachments of the hepatic flexure from the gyrator's fascia, using precise dissection techniques.
The surgeon mobilizes the transverse colon by dividing the gastrocolic omentum and enters the lesser sac to ligate the middle colic vessels.
The surgeon continues the division of the gastrocolic omentum from medial to lateral, carefully avoiding injury to the right gastroepiploic vessels as they come into view.
The right part of the transverse colon is fully mobilized, with the gastro-epiploic vessels preserved. The complete C-loop of the duodenum and the pancreas are visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon divides adhesions within the lesser sac, carefully working close to the stomach wall to prevent injury to the tented mesocolic vessels.
The surgeon completes the division of the gastrocolic omentum on the right side, fully opening the lesser sac. The focus then shifts to the infracolic compartment for further surgical steps.
The surgeon prepares to ligate the middle colic and right colic vessels, highlighting the technical difficulty of this step in the subtotal colectomy procedure.

One of the important things is that excess traction should be avoided at this area because these vessels are directly in line with the supremacentric vein and excess traction may lead to avulsion of the medial colic vein and torrential bleeding from the supremacentric vein.
The branching pattern of the vessels here is extremely variable.

The surgeon examines the middle colic artery, noting its variable branching pattern, which may include right and left branches or occasionally three branches. The division point of these branches can vary in location, either occurring early or higher up.

When we are not operating for a transverse colon malignancy, as in this case, it is better not to try and go too close to the origin of the vessels to avoid any injury to the superior mesentery twain as discussed above.
The surgical field shows a plane cranial to the vessels that has been established during the takedown of the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon exposes the surface of the pancreas by dividing the duodenal colic ligament, revealing the underlying anatomical structures.

The surgeon performs vessel transection higher up from the origin of the middle colic artery, carefully isolating and transecting the right branch of the middle colic artery, the left branch of the middle colic artery, and the right colic artery separately.
The surgeon divides the root of the transverse colon mesentery in an avascular area to gain superior access to the left branch of the middle colic vessels, entering the lesser sac.
The surgeon carefully dissects around the large colonic vessels, clearing fibrous tissue and lymph nodes to ensure a safe length for securing clips and performing a safe transaction.

The surgeon completes the division of the right colic vessels and both branches of the middle colic vessels, followed by the division of loose alar tissue, effectively separating the right colon and hepatic flexure from the duodenal pancreatic complex.
The surgeon performs lateral to medial mobilization of the right colon by dividing its lateral peritoneal attachments, moving the colon medially until it joins the previously created medial plane.

This step is actually done not right now but at the end of the colectomy. I mean after we mobilize the left colon as well so that a mobile right colon doesn't come in the field of dissection but is being demonstrated here for the sake of continuity.

The surgeon emphasizes the importance of having the medial mobilization plane already established during the lateral to medial mobilization of the colon to avoid inadvertently entering the posterior or retroduodenal plane.

The surgeon divides the root of the small bowel mesentery from its attachment to the retroperitoneum, allowing the last part of the small bowel to become freely mobile for positioning into the pelvis for the iliorectal anastomosis.
The surgeon joins the medial and lateral planes of dissection to complete the mobilization of the right colon, ensuring it is fully separated from surrounding structures.
The surgeon frees the terminal ileal mesentery from its retroperitoneal attachment, allowing for increased mobility of the ileum.
The retroperitoneal bed is visible at the completion of the right colon mobilization, revealing the gyrators and the C-loop of the duodenum.
The surgeon begins the medial to lateral mobilization of the left colon by applying traction to the splenic flexure, tenting up the left colic vessels for better visualization and access.

The surgeon divides the peritoneum above and below the left colic vessels, entering the space between the left colonic mesentery and the retroperitoneum. The white line is visible, separating the colonic mesentery from the geratous fascia in the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon divides the peritoneum above the left colic vessel, entering the retroperitoneal plane to continue the dissection.
The video shows a distinct color difference between the yellow colonic mesenteric fat and the gyrator's fat, with a clearly visible white line marking the line of dissection.

When we are doing the left colon and the splenic flexion mobilization, as a part of an LAR it is very important while doing this dissection to avoid any injury to the marginal artery because that ends up as the sole blood vessel on which the left colon vascularity is dependent on.
Of course in this case as we are doing a subtotal colectomy it is not that relevant.
The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, progressively separating the genital structures from the colonic mesentery.
The surgeon separates the genitals and duodenum on the right side, and the tail of the pancreas and genitals on the left side, from the colonic mesentery during mobilization.
The surgeon continues the medial to lateral mobilization of the colon, revealing the bluish surface of the spleen as the dissection progresses.
The surgeon progresses laterally along the retroperitoneal plane during the medial to lateral dissection, dividing the lateral peritoneal attachment of the left colon from the medial side.

The surgeon continues the medial to lateral dissection in the cranial aspect, encountering the distal body and tail of the pancreas as they mobilize the left colon. The duodenum is visible during the right colon mobilization.

In the same way as there is a risk of us going into the retro duodenal plane during the right colon mobilization. Here during the left colon mobilization, there's a risk of us entering the retropancreatic plane as we proceed cranially. So we should be careful not to do that as if we enter the retropancreatic plane, we would be at risk of injuring the splenic vein and the renal vein.
The surgeon ensures positioning on the anterior aspect of the pancreas, dividing the mesocolic attachment and entering the lesser sac anterior to the pancreas.

The surgeon detaches the mesocolic attachment from the anterior aspect of the pancreas, moving from the distal body towards the tail. The retroperitoneal attachment of the splenic flexure is divided while carefully protecting the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon completes the medial to lateral dissection by dividing the left colic vessels. The peritoneum and lymph nodal tissue surrounding the left colic vessels are exposed, and the vessels are secured with double clips and ligated.
The surgeon completes the medial mobilization of the left colon following the division of the left colic artery, ensuring the colon is freed from its medial attachments.
The surgeon separates the distal transverse colon from its omental attachment, proceeding laterally to mobilize the distal transverse colon and splenic flexure by dividing the gastrocolic omentum.

The surgeon divides the linocolic ligament, joining the dissection plane with the previously created retroperitoneal plane from the medial to lateral dissection, completing the mobilization of the left colon.
The surgeon frees the remaining length of the transverse colon by dividing its omental attachment from the left side to the right side, completing the mobilization of the left colon.

The surgeon begins the initial mobilization of the rectum along the Heels plane, preserving the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The lateral peritoneum below the IMA is divided, entering the loose areolar plane behind the IMA. The dissection continues laterally in the total mesorectal excision (TME) Heels plane until the ureter is identified, and the plane is developed inferiorly to the mid rectum. During posterior rectal mobilization, the loose areolar tissue of the TME plane is divided at its superior extent to prevent tenting of the inferior hypogastric plexus nerves, leaving a layer of fibro fatty tissue on the plexus to avoid injury.

Following the completion of the posterior dissection, a gauze piece is placed behind the inferior mesenteric artery and anterior to the identified ureter, ensuring clear visualization and protection of these structures.

The surgeon frees the sigmoid colon by dividing its lateral peritoneal attachments, along with those of the upper rectum. A gauze piece placed anterior to the left ureter guides the dissection, ensuring connection between the planes and maintaining the correct dissection path.

The surgeon completes the mobilization of the sigmoid colon by freeing it from its superior retroperitoneal attachments, connecting it to the already mobilized descending colon. The sigmoid mesentery is then divided as part of the subtotal colectomy procedure.
The surgeon individually ligates and divides the sigmoid vessels arising from the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA), preserving the IMA supply to the rectum, to completely free the sigmoid colon.

The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) and inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) are visible coursing inferiorly, with sigmoid vessels arising perpendicularly towards the sigmoid colon. These vessels are being doubly clipped and subsequently divided.
The surgeon identifies four sigmoid branches arising from the inferior mesenteric artery, which are individually dissected, clipped, and divided while preserving the main trunk of the IMA.
The surgeon completes the dissection up to the colon wall at the proposed site of transection, located at the recto sigmoid junction, following the division of all sigmoid branches.

An on-table colonoscopy is performed to visually confirm that the rectum below the proposed transection site is disease-free. The video shows the completed mobilization of the sigmoid and left colon up to the rectosigmoid junction, with the inferior mesenteric artery preserved.

The surgeon delivers the resected colon specimen through a suprapubic incision. Using a linear cutter stapler, the ileocecal junction and rectosigmoid junction are transected. An ileorectal anastomosis is performed in a side-to-side fashion through the same incision.

The surgeon inspects the completed iliorectal anastomosis and examines the surgical bed for the middle colic, left colic, and sigmoid vascular pedicles. Hemostasis is verified, and a 24 French drain is placed into the pelvis.
The surgeon inspects the middle colic pedicles and the lower border of the pancreas, ensuring clear visualization of these anatomical structures.
The post-operative recovery was uneventful.
The histology revealed haematomatous polyposis of the colon with no evidence of any invasive malignancy.
